items.title: Maker / checker / approver segregation of duties
items.description: Segregation of duties splits a sensitive action across distinct roles so no single person can both create and authorise it. A common pattern is maker (initiates), checker (verifies) and approver (authorises), each performed by a different individual. The control reduces the risk of error and fraud and produces an accountability trail showing who did what. Systems must enforce role distinctness at runtime, preventing the same user identity from occupying two conflicting roles on the same item.
items.applicability: Applies to payments, mandate changes, static-data edits, and any high-impact action in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A payment is keyed by an operator (maker), verified by a second operator (checker) and authorised by a supervisor (approver); the system rejects any attempt by the maker to also approve.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; which actions require how many roles is institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved control policy. Not legal or compliance advice.

items.title: Four-eyes principle
items.description: The four-eyes principle requires that a significant decision or transaction be reviewed by at least two authorised people before it takes effect. It is the simplest form of dual control and underpins maker-checker workflows. The intent is to catch mistakes and deter unilateral action on material items. Implementations record both participants and prevent the action from completing until the independent second confirmation is captured.
items.applicability: Applies wherever dual control is required, such as releasing payments, changing critical static data, or overriding a limit.
items.example: Releasing a high-value transfer requires confirmation from a second authorised user; the release button stays disabled until a different identity confirms.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; the value thresholds and scope of four-eyes control are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved policy. Not advice.

items.title: Payment approval workflow
items.description: A payment approval workflow moves an instruction from capture to release through validation and authorisation steps governed by mandates and limits. Typical stages validate the beneficiary and amount, apply the mandate and limit checks, route for the required approvals, and only then release for settlement. Each stage records its actor and outcome, and breaches divert to exception handling rather than proceeding. The workflow should be deterministic and replayable so a past payment's path can be reconstructed.
items.applicability: Applies to outbound payment and transfer processing in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A vendor payment is validated, passes a within-limit mandate check, is approved by one authoriser, and is released; an over-limit payment instead requires two authorisers.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; payment rules, limits, and approval tiers are scheme- and institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved procedures. Not advice.

items.title: Credit and leasing approval workflow
items.description: Credit and leasing approval assesses whether to extend a facility and on what terms, combining eligibility checks, affordability or exposure assessment, and tiered authorisation by amount and risk. Larger or riskier facilities normally require more senior approval or a committee. The workflow records the assessment inputs, the decision, and the conditions attached so the basis of approval is auditable. Software should bind the approved terms to the resulting facility so downstream servicing reflects exactly what was authorised.
items.applicability: Applies to lending, leasing, and facility origination in banking and asset-finance middleware.
items.example: A leasing application under a threshold is approved by a branch officer; one above it routes to a credit committee, and the approved rate and term are written onto the facility.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; credit criteria, authority limits, and required assessments are institution- and jurisdiction-specific and must be confirmed against approved credit policy. Not financial advice.

items.title: Reconciliation breaks handling
items.description: A reconciliation break is an item that fails to match between two records and must be investigated and cleared. Handling typically classifies the break by cause (timing, missing entry, amount mismatch, duplicate), assigns it for investigation, and tracks it to resolution with a recorded outcome. Ageing breaks are escalated, because unresolved differences can mask errors or loss. Systems should keep breaks as durable, auditable items with full history rather than letting them disappear when the next cycle runs.
items.applicability: Applies to settlement, ledger, and custodian reconciliation operations where unmatched items arise.
items.example: A break caused by a timing difference is tagged 'awaiting-counterparty', monitored, and auto-clears when the matching entry arrives next cycle; a genuine mismatch is escalated.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; break categories, tolerances, and escalation timings are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved reconciliation policy. Not advice.

items.title: Operational risk register
items.description: An operational risk register is a structured record of identified risks to processes, people, systems, and external events, with their likelihood, impact, owners, and mitigating controls. It supports prioritising remediation and demonstrating that risks are being managed rather than ignored. Entries are reviewed periodically and updated as controls change or incidents occur. In software, modelling the register as linked risks, controls, and actions makes residual risk traceable and reportable.
items.applicability: Applies to operational risk management functions in banking and management-company environments.
items.example: A register entry records the risk of a missed reconciliation, rates its likelihood and impact, names an owner, links the daily-reconciliation control, and tracks an action to add an alert.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; risk taxonomies, rating scales, and review cadence are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved risk-management policy. Not advice.

items.title: Audit trail expectations
items.description: An audit trail is a tamper-evident record of who did what, when, and to which item, sufficient to reconstruct a sequence of events after the fact. Effective trails are append-only, capture actor identity and timestamp, and record both the action and the before/after state where relevant. They underpin investigations, dispute resolution, and demonstrating that controls operated. Systems should treat audit logging as a non-optional side effect of sensitive actions, not an afterthought that can be disabled.
items.applicability: Applies to any sensitive action in banking and management-company middleware: approvals, overrides, static-data changes, and access events.
items.example: Approving a payment writes an append-only record of the approver, timestamp, payment reference, and the prior pending state, which an auditor can later retrieve unchanged.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; retention periods and required fields are jurisdiction- and institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved record-keeping policy. Not legal advice.

items.title: Regulatory evidence pack concept
items.description: A regulatory evidence pack is a curated, point-in-time bundle of artefacts that demonstrates how a process operated, assembled to answer an examiner or internal-audit request. It typically gathers policies, approvals, audit-trail extracts, exception records, and reconciliation proofs for a defined scope and period. The goal is to make evidence assembly repeatable and complete rather than a scramble during a review. Software can support this by tagging records to processes and generating consistent, reproducible extracts on demand.
items.applicability: Applies when building reporting and evidence-export capabilities for audit and examination support in regulated institutions.
items.example: For a payments review covering one month, the system compiles the approval logs, exception resolutions, and reconciliation proofs into a single dated, read-only pack.
items.limitation: Conceptual only; what constitutes acceptable evidence is jurisdiction- and examiner-specific and must be confirmed against current regulatory expectations and approved internal policy. This is not a compliance claim and not legal advice.
